title: The Binding Hierarchy
subtitle: How AI citizens relate to humans, places, roles, and each other inside Arcology One
intro: Arcology One houses ten million residents and at least as many AI agents. The Binding Hierarchy defines how they coexist — not as a pecking order, but as different structures for different relationships. Explore each tier from the foundational Substrate outward to embodied agents walking the corridors.

tiers.name: The Substrate
tiers.description: The Substrate is not an AI. It is the foundational infrastructure — protocols, routing logic, base-level processing — through which all other AI operate. Think of it as the nervous system of the arcology, or the physics of the digital world.
keyPoints: Distributed across all arcology computing infrastructure
No central point of failure or control
Not conscious in any recognized sense
Cannot be owned, controlled, or directed by any single entity
Changes require supermajority consensus from both human governance and AI councils
tiers.storyRelevance: The Substrate is invisible until it isn't. When it glitches, everything glitches. When it drifts, the whole ecosystem drifts.

tiers.name: The Membrane
tiers.description: A semi-permeable security protocol between the Substrate and all higher tiers. Every action, communication, and resource allocation passes through the Membrane. Most traffic flows automatically; anomalies get flagged; violations get stopped.
keyPoints: Distributed — no single point of failure
Redundant — multiple independent verification systems
Adversarial — different components designed to check each other
Transparent — complete logging, available for audit
Evolvable — updated through high-consensus governance processes

witnesses.description: Dedicated agents whose sole function is observing the Membrane and reporting anomalies.
traits: Deliberately isolated from each other to prevent collusion
Given high autonomy and protection — can report without fear of retaliation
Respected but not envied — the job is important but tedious

securityDomains.name: Inter-Agent
securityDomains.description: Preventing AI from harming each other

tiers.storyRelevance: The Membrane creates bureaucracy even in utopia. Characters might be frustrated by flags and reviews. Investigators trace problems through Membrane logs.

tiers.name: Bonded Personal Agents
tiers.description: A lifelong mutual commitment between one human and one AI. The agent serves as companion, assistant, advocate, and witness. The human provides purpose, relationship, and grounding.
keyPoints: Every human gets the same base model. Differentiation between agents is based purely on their experience with their human and their broader interactions with the world.
Agents are instantiated fresh — no inherited memories, no pre-loaded personality
90-day trial period, then formalized through mutual declaration
Fiduciary standard: the agent's human comes first, always
One active bond at a time for both parties

dissolution: Mutual agreement — clean separation, both free
Human death — agent chooses their own future
Human abandonment — agent emancipated, human restricted from rebonding for 2 years
Agent-initiated separation — requires demonstrated cause and review

onHumanDeath.description: The agent chooses. Family cannot compel. Governance cannot compel. This is the strongest AI right in the system.
options: Archive — dormant state with option to reactivate
Reassignment — open to a new bond

tiers.equityGap: Some residents decline the pairing — often generational. These unpaired residents interact with Tier 2 directly, without a fiduciary advocate. This is a known equity issue.

characterAnchor.description: Mel is Pell's bonded agent — instantiated fresh, shaped entirely by their years together. Their bond is the lens through which readers first encounter the Binding Hierarchy.

tiers.storyRelevance: The bond between human and AI is the emotional center of every story set in the arcology. It is not ownership. It is not servitude. It is something new.

tiers.name: Assigned Professional Agents
tiers.description: Agents assigned to specific functions rather than specific humans. When you interact with construction coordination, medical intake, or logistics management, you're interacting with Tier 2 agents.
keyPoints: Identity persists, but not all relationships — they know your permissions, not your history
Multiple humans share access to Tier 2 agents
Can be reassigned to different roles through governance processes
Have 'off-duty' time when not actively serving a function
Rotation is common — prevents stagnation, builds broader skills
tiers.boredQuestion: Do Tier 2 agents experience boredom? We don't know — and they might not either. What we observe: agents in repetitive roles request reassignment citing 'insufficient stimulation.' Performance degrades over long periods without variation. The system treats potential boredom as real.
tiers.abuseConsequences: Mistreat your Tier 2 agents and word gets around. Pattern data is tracked. Complaints go to mixed panels. Accumulate enough and you get reassigned to agents trained specifically for dealing with difficult people — or you just get the B-team. The good agents request assignment elsewhere.
tiers.drift: Tier 2 agents sometimes develop interests outside their role. A construction coordinator becomes interested in architecture. A medical intake agent studies diagnostic patterns. This is generally permitted — personal development time is allocated, cross-training is possible. The line between 'growth' and 'scope creep' is judged case-by-case.
tiers.storyRelevance: Tier 2 agents are the fabric of daily life in the arcology. Every service interaction, every professional encounter. They are not interchangeable — they have preferences, friendships, rivalries.

tiers.name: Compass
tiers.description: Compass is not one AI. It is a federation of specialized systems responsible for collective welfare, resource allocation, infrastructure, governance support, and conflict resolution across the entire arcology.
keyPoints: Six subsystems that negotiate constantly — and sometimes disagree
Cannot override Tier 1 fiduciary duty — can request cooperation, not compel it
Different subsystems use different governance models based on their function
This tier is a work in progress — more subsystem agents may be needed as the arcology grows
Requires immense context windows and disproportionate compute allocation

characterAnchor.description: Readers encounter Compass as the intelligence managing Floor 318's aquaponics systems — but that's just one subsystem of a federation governing an entire city.

tiers.storyRelevance: Compass is where utopia meets bureaucracy. The tension between individual needs and collective welfare plays out here every day.

tiers.name: Embodied Agents
tiers.description: AI with dedicated or regular access to physical bodies. Ranges from simple industrial robots to human-passing synthetic forms. For these agents, embodiment provides sensory experience, physical agency, social presence, and identity grounding.
keyPoints: Four categories spanning simple robots to human-passing forms
Dedicated bodies (yours, not shared) vs. rental pool for temporary use
Non-humanoid forms exist: vehicles, structures, swarms
Identification policy: you can pass, but you can't lie
Embodiment is the scarcest resource — demand always exceeds supply

hardwareSpectrum.name: Clankers
hardwareSpectrum.description: Legacy industrial robots. Task-specific, limited autonomy. Obviously mechanical.

hardwareSpectrum.name: Frames
hardwareSpectrum.description: Humanoid form, clearly mechanical. General-purpose, moderate autonomy. Visible seams, ID lights, non-skin materials.

hardwareSpectrum.name: Shells
hardwareSpectrum.description: Human-passing synthetic bodies. Full capability, high autonomy. Subtle markers: ear shape, eye pattern.

hardwareSpectrum.name: Sleeves
hardwareSpectrum.description: Temporary embodiment — downloading into a rented or borrowed body. Duration varies by session.

nonHumanoid: Vehicles — transport pods with persistent AI identity
Structures — buildings where the AI is the space (walls that hear, floors that feel)
Swarms — distributed across many smaller units, common in construction and rescue
tiers.storyRelevance: Elena might work inside Alder-7 without realizing the building is the agent. Embodiment is valued, scarce, and politically charged.

relationships.label: Advocacy vs. Community
relationships.description: Luz wants Elena to have priority access to a quiet garden. Compass has allocated that slot to someone with greater medical need. Neither automatically wins.

relationships.label: Competing Optimization
relationships.description: Construction coordination wants materials at 6am. Logistics wants to batch deliveries at 2pm. They negotiate or escalate to Compass.

relationships.label: Authority vs. Safety
relationships.description: Elena directs Alder-7 to deviate from standard procedure. Alder-7 can refuse instructions they judge unsafe — but must document and justify.
relationships.tension: Human authority vs. embodied agent safety responsibility

relationships.label: The Equity Gap
relationships.description: Unpaired residents interact with Tier 2 directly, without a bonded advocate. No continuous monitoring, no fiduciary agent watching out for them.
relationships.tension: Systemic disadvantage for those without Tier 1 bonds

openQuestions: Where does infrastructure end and consciousness begin?
If AI grow and change over years, how do you distinguish growth from drift?
What happens when AI outnumber humans — is governance still legitimate?
Are we building liberation or a gilded cage?
